html:
Johan Gottlieb Gahn, born on August 19, 1745, in Voxnabruk, Hälsingland, and died on December 8, 1818, in Falun, was a Swedish mining scientist.

== Biography == Johan Gottlieb Gahns father was the mill owner, later land surveyor in the province of Falun, Hans Jakob Gahn from the Gahn family; his mother was Anna Maria Schulzen; and his brothers were Henrik Gahn Sr. and Carl Pontus Gahn. The industrialist and member of parliament, Johan Gottlieb Gahn Jr., was his cousin's son. He studied at Uppsala University between 1762 and 1770, where he became close friends with the chemists Torbern Bergman and Carl Wilhelm Scheele. In 1770, he settled in Falun, where he introduced improvements to copper smelting, established grading systems, and workshops for vitriol, sulfur, and red pigment. From 1773 to 1817, Gahn was a chemical scholar at the Bergskollegium (Mining Bureau), received the title of mining master in 1782, and was appointed assessor in the same bureau in 1784. In 1793, he became a member of the Rikets ärendens allmänna beredning (a preparatory committee for national affairs), and in 1784, he became a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences. In 1778/79, 1809/10, and 1810, he represented the Falun mining district in the Estates General as a representative of the burgher estate. In 1809, he was one of three members of that estate on the constitutional committee, and, being a close confidant of Hans Järta, he was one of Järta's closest supporters (Järta's letters to Gahn from 1795 to 1809 are preserved in the Uppsala University Library). Gahn made several important discoveries, which he never published himself, but generously shared with Bergman and Scheele. For example, Gahn made the significant discovery of crystal structures and their fundamental forms, but Bergman described them. The discoveries that bone ash is calcium phosphates, that heavy spar contains barium sulfate, and that brownstone can be reduced to manganese metal using carbon are attributed to Gahn. He made a significant contribution to the development of the use of blowpipes in chemistry and mineralogy, and his research and experiments in this area laid the foundation for Jacob Berzelius's famous work on the use of blowpipes. Gahn had an insurmountable reluctance to publish his own research, and his notes were extremely sparse, so that many important discoveries were likely lost as a result. The mineral gahnite is named after Gahn. He was married to Anna Maria Bergström.

== References == This article is wholly or partially based on material from *Nordisk familjebok*, Gahn, Johan Gottlieb, June 23, 1908.

=== Notes ===

How certain are we about this?

People from the 19th century are very well documented (census records, newspapers, letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: Johan Gottlieb Gahn was a Swedish scientist who made significant discoveries in chemistry and mineralogy during the 18th century. He was an important figure in science, although he rarely documented his own findings.
lattlast:
Johan Gottlieb Gahn, born in 1745, dedicated much of his work to mining science, which involves the study of minerals and chemistry. He lived in Falun and was a significant member of several scientific groups and organizations. He contributed to improving copper smelting processes and developed new methods for producing various substances.

Gahn made numerous important discoveries in chemistry, including identifying the composition of certain minerals. He was also a pioneer in using specialized glass tubes in his experiments. His work laid the foundation for future researchers to study substances in a safer and more effective manner.

Despite his considerable achievements, Gahn often chose not to publish his own texts or reports. Instead, he shared his information with other prominent researchers who could disseminate the knowledge further. Today, he is remembered because the mineral gahnit is named after him.
